Section 4.2(b)'s reporting-threshold approach (models trained above 10^26 floating-point/integer operations, computing clusters above defined thresholds) creates an incentive for developers to engineer training runs just below the numeric threshold to avoid reporting obligations, rather than to build genuinely safer systems -- a specific, threshold-gaming perverse-incentive structure.

title: Advancing United States Leadership in Artificial Intelligence Infrastructure

flags.justification: Creates an unprecedented direct government-private partnership mechanism in which the Departments of Defense and Energy lease Federal land to private AI developers under extensive Federal control conditions -- national-security vetting of investors and investment sources (Section 4(h)(vi)), mandatory MOUs with NIST's AI Safety Institute for model evaluation (Section 4(h)(v)), and a requirement that the Federal Government receive access to resulting AI models and derivative resources 'at terms at least no less favorable than current market rates' (Section 4(h)(vii)) -- a genuinely novel assertion of executive control over private AI infrastructure investment and operation.

Extensive 'as appropriate and consistent with applicable law' hedging throughout Sections 4-9; non-Federal parties self-report investment sources and compliance with no independent verification mechanism specified.

Bundles Federal land-siting policy, clean-energy procurement and geothermal/nuclear deployment, national-security investment vetting, NEPA permitting reform (new categorical exclusions), transmission-infrastructure development, semiconductor/grid-equipment supply-chain policy, labor standards, and international AI-infrastructure diplomacy into a single eleven-section order -- a breadth of bundling rivaling Executive Order 14110 itself, though concentrated on physical/energy infrastructure rather than software governance.

Site-selection, permitting, and procurement criteria throughout Sections 4-9 are governed overwhelmingly by 'as appropriate,' 'as feasible,' and 'to the extent consistent with applicable law' qualifiers.

Section 4(h)(iii) requires non-Federal parties to bear 'all responsibility for paying any costs,' yet the Federal Government simultaneously provides preferential site access, accelerated permitting, and categorical-exclusion fast-tracking -- creating an incentive for developers to seek Federal-site placement primarily for regulatory acceleration rather than genuine open-market competition.
Not applicable - the order's requirements are standing/indefinite rather than a time-limited grant of new authority.

Section 4(g)(iv)-(vii)'s national-security review and investment-source vetting is administered entirely internally by the Secretaries of Defense and Energy (in consultation with the Director of National Intelligence) with no independent or judicial review mechanism specified.

Section 4(h)(vi)'s investment/financial-capital reporting requirement relies on non-Federal party self-reporting for national-security risk evaluation, with no independent verification mechanism built into the order.

Explicitly builds on and cites Executive Order 14110 of October 30, 2023 (this stream: 70.0%, Chunk 6) as its foundational AI-competition policy, Executive Order 14126 of September 6, 2024 (this stream: 44.44%, Chunk 6) for construction labor standards, and Executive Order 14028 of May 12, 2021 (this stream: 37.5%) for cybersecurity standards, plus National Security Memorandum 25 -- forming a dense four-instrument policy-dependency web that deepens the AI-governance architecture established by EO14110 just eleven weeks earlier.

DOD, DOE, Interior, Commerce, EPA, USACE, the Federal Permitting Improvement Steering Council, and CEQ all separately administer overlapping siting, permitting, and environmental-review responsibilities across the order's many sections, with the pre-existing 'White House Task Force on AI Datacenter Infrastructure' (Section 9(h)) noted in passing as yet another coordinating body alongside the three AI-coordination structures already created by EO14110.

Section 7(b)(iii) and Section 9(b) both direct identification and adoption of new NEPA categorical exclusions specifically to accelerate AI-infrastructure permitting -- a genuine, deliberately constructed regulatory-exemption mechanism.

title: Strengthening and Promoting Innovation in the Nation's Cybersecurity

flags.justification: Section 3(c) creates significant new CISA authority to gain direct, timely access to individual agencies' endpoint-detection-and-response data and security-operations-center telemetry -- a real expansion of a central agency's technical reach into other agencies' networks.

Section 2(b)'s software-attestation regime creates an incentive for vendors to submit complete-looking but unverified attestations, since CISA validates only a sample of submissions rather than every attestation.

Section 3(c)(iii) permits CISA to make changes to an agency's network or system 'unless otherwise authorized by the agency,' and Section 8's National Security Systems carve-out removes an entire category of Federal systems from external application of the order's requirements, governed instead by an internal National Security Memorandum process.
Section 2's software-attestation regime and Section 7(c)'s contractor cybersecurity-practice requirements both rely substantially on self-reported compliance data.

Section 9 directly amends Section 1(a) of Executive Order 13694 of April 1, 2015 (this stream: 50.0%) -- itself already amended by Executive Order 13757 of December 28, 2016 (this stream: 44.44%) and further amended by Executive Order 13984 of January 19, 2021 (this stream: 37.5%) -- making this the FOURTH order in the same cyber-emergency lineage, adding entirely new blocking-designation categories (election-process interference, ransomware attacks) absent from the original 2015 criteria.
